skill.name: yescan-transoffice-universal
skill.description: Convert images, screenshots, and scans into editable Word, Excel, or PDF documents using Quark Scan OCR service.
skill.summary: Image-to-Office and image-to-PDF conversion skill powered by Quark Scan, preserving complex tables, contracts, and mixed layouts.
skill.author: yescan-ai

security_audit.summary: After evaluation, the vast majority of static findings are false positives triggered by Chinese UTF-8 documentation text matching patterns like 'backtick', 'weak', and 'credential'. The skill is a legitimate image-to-Office/PDF conversion tool that calls the documented Quark Scan OCR API (scan-business.quark.cn). The network calls, filesystem access, and environment variable reads are all core to the stated functionality. No prompt injection or malicious intent was detected. The medium risk rating reflects that the skill sends user-provided images to a third-party service and requires an API key, which warrants user awareness.

medium_findings.title: Outbound network calls to third-party OCR service
medium_findings.description: The skill sends user-provided images to scan-business.quark.cn via HTTPS POST requests (scripts/common/ocr_client.py:203). This is the documented core functionality (Quark Scan OCR API), not data exfiltration. Users should be aware their images are processed by a third-party service. API key is sent in the request body for authentication.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Direct evidence of POST requests to a documented OCR endpoint with the API key in the body. This is intentional, documented behavior matching the skill's purpose.

medium_findings.title: API key read from environment or dotfile
medium_findings.description: The skill reads SCAN_WEBSERVICE_KEY from the SCAN_WEBSERVICE_KEY environment variable or ~/.yescan_env file (scripts/common/ocr_client.py:50-56). This is the skill's own authentication credential, not arbitrary secret harvesting. The dotfile path is documented in the privacy reference.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Standard credential loading pattern for API client skills. The key is only used to authenticate to the documented OCR endpoint, not exfiltrated.

low_findings.title: Base64 validation of user input
low_findings.description: scripts/common/ocr_client.py:150 calls base64.b64decode() to validate user-supplied base64 data. This is input validation, not obfuscation or malicious decoding. The result is sent to the OCR service as the image payload.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: base64.b64decode is called inside a try/except for validation. No shell execution or dynamic code evaluation follows.

low_findings.title: Local file read with expanduser
low_findings.description: scripts/common/ocr_client.py:160 calls os.path.expanduser on user-provided file paths before reading. This resolves ~ to home directory. FileValidator.validate() is called before read. No path traversal vulnerability observed.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: File read is preceded by FileValidator.validate(). The file content is base64-encoded and sent to the documented OCR API.

content.user_title: Convert Images to Editable Office Documents
content.value_statement: Transform scanned documents, screenshots, and photos into editable Word, Excel, or PDF files. The skill uses Quark Scan OCR to preserve complex tables, contracts, and mixed layouts that simpler OCR tools cannot handle.
actual_capabilities: Convert images, screenshots, and scans to editable .docx Word documents
Convert images containing tables and data to .xlsx Excel spreadsheets
Convert images to PDF format preserving original layout
Preserve complex table structures during conversion
Accept input as URL, local file path, or base64-encoded data
Return conversion results as local file paths in the system temp directory
limitations: Only processes a single image per request; batch processing is not supported
Maximum image size is 5MB per file
Supports only static image formats: jpg, jpeg, png, gif, bmp, webp, tiff, wbmp
Requires a valid SCAN_WEBSERVICE_KEY API key configured in ~/.yescan_env or environment
Images are sent to a third-party OCR service (scan-business.quark.cn) for processing

use_cases.title: Digitize scanned contracts
use_cases.description: Convert paper contract scans into editable Word documents while preserving formatting and layout.

use_cases.title: Extract tables from screenshots
use_cases.description: Transform screenshots containing financial data, reports, or spreadsheets into editable Excel files.
use_cases.target_user: Financial analysts and data entry clerks

use_cases.title: Convert photos to PDF
use_cases.description: Turn photographed documents, receipts, or whiteboards into searchable PDF files.

prompt_templates.title: Basic image to Word
prompt_templates.prompt: Convert this image to an editable Word document: https://example.com/contract-scan.png
prompt_templates.scenario: User has a scanned contract or document image and wants an editable .docx file

prompt_templates.title: Table extraction to Excel
prompt_templates.prompt: Extract the table from this screenshot and save it as Excel: https://example.com/sales-report.png
prompt_templates.scenario: User needs tabular data from a screenshot converted to .xlsx with preserved structure

prompt_templates.title: Photo to PDF conversion
prompt_templates.prompt: Convert this photo of a receipt to a PDF file: /Users/user/Documents/receipt.jpg
prompt_templates.scenario: User has a local image file on disk and wants PDF output

prompt_templates.title: Batch-ready single conversion
prompt_templates.prompt: I have a scanned form at /tmp/form.png. Please convert it to Word format, preserving all field labels and table layouts.
prompt_templates.scenario: User needs precise control over conversion quality and format preservation

output: The skill identifies the image-to-excel scene
Executes the OCR API call with the image URL
Receives a base64-encoded .xlsx file in the response
Saves the file to the system temp directory
Returns the file path to the user for download

output_examples.input: User: Turn this scan into a Word doc: /home/user/letter.png
output: The skill identifies the image-to-word scene
Reads the local file and encodes it to base64
Sends the encoded image to the OCR service
Returns the file path to the user

output_examples.input: User: Make a PDF from https://example.com/document.png
output: The skill identifies the image-to-pdf scene
Calls the OCR API with the image URL
Saves it to the system temp directory
Returns the file path to the user

best_practices: Ensure images are clear and high-resolution for best OCR accuracy on complex tables
Configure the SCAN_WEBSERVICE_KEY in ~/.yescan_env before invoking the skill to avoid repeated setup
Keep image files under 5MB to stay within API size limits
anti_patterns: Do not use this skill for plain text extraction or pure OCR without document conversion
Do not attempt batch processing of multiple images in a single request
Do not use the skill for video frame processing or real-time camera streams

faq.question: What image formats are supported?
faq.answer: The skill supports jpg, jpeg, png, gif, bmp, webp, tiff, and wbmp formats. Video files and other formats are not supported.

faq.question: How do I get an API key?
faq.answer: Visit scan.quark.cn/business, register or log in, and find your API key in the developer dashboard. Store it in the SCAN_WEBSERVICE_KEY environment variable or in ~/.yescan_env.

faq.question: Is my data sent to a third party?
faq.answer: Yes. Images are sent to the Quark Scan OCR service (scan-business.quark.cn) for processing. The service is operated by Quark. Do not use this skill for highly sensitive or classified documents.

faq.question: What is the maximum image size?
faq.answer: The API accepts images up to 5MB. For larger images, compress or resize them before submitting.

faq.question: Can I convert multiple images at once?
faq.answer: No. Each request processes a single image. For batch conversion, call the skill repeatedly with each image individually.

faq.question: Where are the output files saved?
faq.answer: Converted files are saved to the system temporary directory (typically /tmp on Linux/macOS or %TEMP% on Windows). The skill returns the full file path in the response.
